chapters.title: Positive thinking is vital for healing
chapters.text:
Becoming more aware of the events that occurred and did not occur throughout childhood is essential to understand yourself better. What you don’t remember since what didn’t happen has just as much influence over you as things that did. You’ll be exposed to the repercussions of what didn’t happen. You’ll get a better understanding of how to determine if you have been the victim of emotional neglect and, if so, how to recover.

Emotions are the basis of life. If we weren't happy as children, we won’t be satisfied as adults.

Many individuals who seem high-functioning and intellectually healthy on the surface are internally unsatisfied or alienated from life in various ways. They are perplexed about why they aren’t pleased or feel unfulfilled despite doing everything correctly. Questions about a lack of emotional support torment this group of individuals. They feel responsible for everything.

Emotional neglect by parents or guardians is a regular occurrence that happens daily in homes and families across the globe. We fail to recognize its existence or the way it influences our lives. Usually, there isn’t a name for it. It’s intangible but always present. And if you can’t explain or name what you’re experiencing, you can’t eliminate it. What these feelings need is recognition. Once you understand them, you’ll find it easier to find solutions to your problems. Each wound can be healed or at least made less painful. You can fix the damage inflicted on you with enough effort and determination. With this knowledge, you’ll get a better understanding of yourself and people around you. You’ll find out what resources you need to build healthy relationships. You’ll learn to forgive yourself and choose kindness over guilt.

chapters.title: It doesn’t take a parenting expert to raise a happy, healthy adult
chapters.text:
A child's emotional development is inevitably interconnected with the relationship they have with their parents. Without this connection, the child grows into an adult who suffers emotionally; the child may look happy on the outside, but they’ll lack love, support, and attention within.

How exactly do parents influence the emotional development of their children? The Attachment Theory is a good way to understand this correlation. It says, young children need to bond with at least one primary caregiver for optimal social and emotional development. This theory helps us understand the role of emotion in parenting. Parents provide our emotional needs for safety and connection.

A secure attachment to a parent is intrinsic to a child's happiness.

What ensures the creation of a healthy attachment? There are three key points:

• There’s a strong emotional bond.
• The parent focuses on the child and views them as unique instead of an extension of themselves.
• The child feels that their emotional needs are met.

These rules seem simple enough, but they are vital for creating a secure emotional bond and giving a child sufficient skills to understand and manage their emotions. Then, they can face the world with healthy emotional habits. Some parents have a natural ability to accomplish this, while others need a bit more work. Fortunately, the child will not be ignored in either case.

Imagine that one parent teaches a child to feel shame if they need or ask for attention. In that case, they will grow up oblivious to many of their own psychological responses. They won’t know how to build healthy relationships, they will repress their emotions and needs, and they will feel miserable. Emotionally neglected kids grow into sensitive adults who feel guilty for experiencing intense feelings. They don’t know how to communicate their emotions and find it difficult to create strong and healthy bonds with other people.

Did you know? The 2020 United States Census Bureau states that 18.3 million children, or one in every four, don't have an active father, be it biological, adoptive or step.

chapters.title: The invisible consequences of emotional neglect
chapters.text:
Humans are under the influence of their conscious and subconscious past. Emotionally flawed people have no clue how their upbringing influenced them. Instead, they prefer to blame themselves for life’s challenges. They ask questions like, “Why are others happier than me?” “Why don’t I feel closer to my family?” “What am I missing?” They hide their emptiness well, making it difficult for others to detect. Only their closest friends and family get a peek into their thought processes. They may also suffer from these emotional states:

Depression

Few people seek therapy since they are unaware that they are depressed in the first place. It’s not as striking as other disorders like anxiety, so sometimes it’s more difficult to notice. Depression is akin to feeling nothing at all, a complete void. Some feel emptiness in their stomach or chest, while others report emotional numbness.

Counter-dependence

Dependence is well-known. However, few have heard the term “counter-dependence.” Mental health experts mainly utilize it. It means to be independent or, more precisely, to be afraid of becoming reliant on another person. Emotionally neglected people are frequently terrified of seeming needy. They develop avoidant tendencies to keep themselves from relying on other people. Because of this, they can’t form strong emotional bonds with others; they are afraid of being hurt or neglected again.

Lack of self-pity

Compassion is a noble feeling. It’s vital for understanding others and creating bonds. Emotionally neglected people tend to have a lot of compassion for others — sometimes too much. They can be too tolerant of others’ behavior or forgive too quickly. It’s second nature to make excuses for people who hurt them. Emotionally neglected people try to find reasons for how people behave, not allowing themselves to rage or be upset for too long. What they lack is compassion for themselves. Their self-image is frequently damaged, so they treat themselves harshly, expecting too much and raging when making the tiniest mistakes.

Getting favorable attention is crucial for children but might be challenging for emotionally closed-off parents.

Validation and recognition are intrinsic to the child’s emotional development. Without them, they won’t manage their feelings as adults. As an adult, they may have limited tolerance for strong feelings or any emotions. They may be furious, upset, anxious, irritated, or even joyful, and blame themselves for it.

chapters.title: Parental neglect is a huge enabler of depression and suicide
chapters.text:
Suicide is not an easy subject, but it’s vital to talk about it. Some don’t understand it — after all, trying to escape death is the human condition, so suicide seems an unnatural and irrational act. But we must realize that people don’t end their lives for no reason.

When a child receives the message, even subtly or indirectly, that his emotions don't matter, he will grow up feeling, somewhere deep inside, that he himself doesn't matter. ~ Jonice Webb, PhD

Suicide might be a response to a terrible event after which a person just can’t continue. Sometimes people end their lives because they want to escape the consequences of their actions. Mental illnesses, such as bipolar disorder or depression, frequently lead to suicide. Either way, finding reasons for this act is necessary to prevent such decisions in the future.

Emotional neglect is a sure proponent of depression. Learning to deal with feelings is a vital skill that people should acquire as soon as possible in their lives. Being overwhelmed with emotions is just as bad as feeling nothing at all. Both may push a person to end their life. However, if individuals know how to comprehend and appreciate their feelings, they will have enough willpower to keep themselves from making drastic decisions. They won’t be afraid to ask for help if they find themselves in a dark space — this skill is vital. In addition, they won’t feel guilt or shame when experiencing intense emotions.

An excellent way to avoid suicidal thoughts is to accept life’s unpredictability and prepare for tough times in advance.

Parents inadvertently harm their children’s self-esteem by failing to engage in their achievements fully. For parental attention, some youngsters misbehave or take drugs. Others grow up with low self-esteem and don’t know why. They blame themselves for their internal issues. Low self-esteem in children can lead to embarrassment and guilt, which may later cause sadness, leading to depression and, sometimes, suicide. Therefore, showing your kid that they are valid no matter what is essential for raising a human being with a good sense of self.

chapters.title: Even the simplest things can become obstacles
chapters.text:
It’s possible to fail if you’re too expectant or too scared. But success results from continuous hard work; if you cannot remain consistent, it will be difficult to see positive changes in your life. Some things that hinder your progress might include:

• Unrealistic expectations: It’s normal to assume that success will gradually build upon itself once you start working on a change, improving over time. But real change seldom works that way; it occurs in stages. You must teach yourself to accept steps backward to move forward.
• Development: Change is difficult for many people. First, you must force yourself to do something unfamiliar; second, you must be persistent; and third, making a change takes a lot of effort.

Avoidance is a typical response under such conditions. It’s much less difficult to ignore your problems than to fight them, right? However, much like self-anger, avoidance hinders development. Avoidance may seem like a good solution, but it only gives you temporary respite. The only way to overcome it is to face it. Recognize when your avoidance kicks in, then confront it. Remind yourself that this won’t lead you anywhere. Everything worthwhile takes lots of hard work.

There are no “bad” emotions or feelings. We just have to learn how to approach each of them.

Now that you’ve identified your possible challenges or problems, it’s time to understand what changes might help you heal and recover:

• Value your emotions. It’s a widespread misconception that smart people aren’t emotional. Intelligent individuals utilize emotions to think and ideas to control feelings. It's all about your spirit. Use your feelings to better your life.
• Empathize with others. You need to recognize and communicate your feelings. You manage your emotions when you identify and name them. This allows your brain to process your condition and proffer a solution.
• Monitor your emotions. Putting emotions into words takes practice. It is a talent that must be developed, just like any other.
• Trust your feelings. Emotional neglect might have taught you to mistrust your feelings. Others suppress their emotions. They see their feelings as a burden, equating experiencing overwhelming emotions to being a terrible person who wants too much. Some people frequently ignore their feelings. Feeling guilty for being overwhelmed is a standard routine for emotionally neglected people.

chapters.title: Positive thinking is vital for healing
chapters.text:
Those who were emotionally neglected in childhood can heal in four fundamental ways when it comes to self-care and self-needs. These four ways are:

1. Self-care: Taking care of yourself is vital for feeling happy and healthy. Emotional neglect might have taught you to ignore your needs and prioritize the feelings of others. You must break this pattern and learn to be kinder to yourself. Self-care will also positively influence people around you, showing them a healthy mindset. Here are four examples of how you can take care of yourself:

• Step A: Prioritizing yourself
• Step B: Eating well
• Step C: Exercising
• Step D: Relaxation

2. Improving self-discipline: Many individuals who have been emotionally ignored openly admit to being procrastinators. But procrastination, laziness, overeating, undereating, excessive spending, and excessive drinking should all be avoided.
3. Paying attention to your emotions: You’re probably used to suppressing your feelings. Though it might be scary, try facing them. Find the reasons behind them, if possible. Once you recognize your emotions, living through them will be much easier. If you don’t feel brave enough to face everything you feel just yet, come up with the things that might distract you and make you comfortable.

Repressed feelings frequently have too much impact on us. Acknowledging them can lessen their power over our lives.

4. Self-soothing: Regardless of how well you manage your feelings, there will be moments when you are emotionally unhappy. Life, as you know, is full of surprises. And you’ll have feelings based on those experiences. When you’re feeling overwhelmed, try doing something that brings you joy. For example, you can walk, play games, or visit friends.
5. Self-compassion: Don’t be too hard on yourself when you make mistakes. The same level of pity you have for others when they falter is the same level of compassion you should have for yourself.

When you combine these four steps, you’ll experience a shift in your life. You’ll become less worried about things, which will help you live a happier and freer life.

Do unto your child as you wish your parents had done unto you. ~ Jonice Webb, PhD

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Emotions, incredibly intense and sudden ones, are frequently too complicated to comprehend. People need specific skills to recognize their feelings, name them, understand their causes or triggers, and deal with them. People who experience emotional neglect tend to suppress their emotions, letting them bottle up and make them miserable. You can draw on your emotions, perceiving them as your strengths. To achieve this, you have to treat yourself with kindness. You deserve this. Emotions, no matter which, bring life and make you human. Unfortunately, this critical built-in feedback system has been suppressed, ignored, driven underground, and ridiculed for far too long.

Parenting is one of the most rewarding experiences, and you should treasure the chance to do it. The need to provide a better life for your children, regardless of the circumstances you find yourself in, is a biological and social duty. We must reorganize our expectations and spend more time and attention on our children than our parents did when we were youngsters. This will allow us to give our children advantages we could not provide for ourselves. When we do this, we will ensure that our children have a future where they will not feel unwanted or ignored. Even if it’s too late for us to go back and change our childhoods, it’s never too late to gather our experiences and use them to save the younger generation; together, we can heal and prevent emotional neglect.

Try this

Try to retain as many happy moments as possible. Whenever you’re sad or lonely, return to these happy memories and identify the things you did that made you happy. You should also avoid being too hard on yourself because you’re the only one who can forgive yourself.
